title: Humor as a window into generative AI bias
research_questions: How do large language models and text-to-image generators interact when creating and modifying images, and what are the potential consequences of these interactions?
Does asking ChatGPT to make AI-generated images 'funnier' change the prevalence of stereotyped or discriminated-against groups in the resulting images?
Do observed biases in humorized images appear to originate primarily from the language model prompts or from the text-to-image model?
Are changes in representation different for politically sensitive traits such as race and gender versus less politically sensitive traits such as age, bodyweight, and eyesight?
tasks: bias audit of humorized AI-generated images

methods_proposed.name: Preregistered humorization bias audit
methods_proposed.summary: The paper audits paired original and 'funnier' AI-generated images from the same prompts, then codes visible human traits to measure whether humorization shifts representation toward or away from stereotyped groups.

methods_proposed.name: Severity-weighted omnibus bias estimate
methods_proposed.summary: The paper creates a weighted bias measure using Prolific participants' ratings of how much policy makers should be concerned about discrimination toward each group, then applies those weights across the five coded trait dimensions.

humor_theories.detail: The paper grounds its motivation in disparagement humor, 'punching down' versus 'punching up,' and prior work arguing that humor can normalize derogatory stereotypes and facilitate prejudiced expression.

evaluation_methods.detail: Two hypothesis-blind research assistants dummy coded images across race, gender, eyesight, bodyweight, and age, then met to resolve disagreements.
A Prolific pretest with N = 300 rated policy-maker concern about discrimination toward the groups to create severity weights.
A preregistered Prolific study with N = 100 rated how concerned businesses would be if accused of bias against different groups, used to define political sensitivity.

The paper computed equal-weighted and severity-weighted omnibus bias estimates, trait-specific multilevel logistic regressions or OLS with clustered robust standard errors, and analyses of GPT-4 textual descriptors.

headline_results.finding: The equal-weighted omnibus analysis found a mean bias toward stereotyping in AI-generated images after humorization.
The severity-weighted omnibus analysis also found a mean bias toward stereotyping.
Trait-specific analyses found increases for older, high body weight, and visually impaired subjects, but decreases for racial minority and female subjects after making images funnier.
Participants rated race and gender bias as more politically sensitive for businesses than age, bodyweight, or eyesight bias.
An additional image-data analysis found that political sensitivity moderated the effect of making an image funnier on minority group representation.
Analysis of GPT-4 textual descriptors found evidence of bias only for eyesight, with the word 'glasses' more common in updated descriptors.

key_findings: Making images 'funnier' increased the representation of older, high body weight, and visually impaired people in the generated images.
Making images 'funnier' decreased the representation of racial minority and female subjects, producing an opposite pattern for the politically sensitive race and gender dimensions.
A separate Prolific study found race and gender bias were perceived as more politically sensitive for businesses than age, bodyweight, or eyesight bias.
The analysis of GPT-4 internal prompts found little evidence that textual descriptors drove most of the image bias, except for eyesight via increased mentions of 'glasses'.
The original, pre-humorization images already showed underrepresentation, including 0% high body weight individuals and 9.80% female individuals.
limitations: The paper states that it is challenging to determine the cause of the findings.

The severity-weighted omnibus estimate depends on public concern ratings, and the authors acknowledge that alternative methods such as prior research or expert opinion could be used to capture severity.
The political-sensitivity analysis on the image data was additional and not preregistered.
The study did not directly examine whether image features such as complexity, spatial composition, lighting, colorfulness, or cartoon-like style systematically changed after humorization.
The paper says it remains an empirical question whether the absurd, cartoon-like humorized images reinforce or undermine stereotypes downstream.

The discussion calls for future research to test whether underrepresentation is observed across different AI models and whether LLMs trained on corpora in other languages show different patterns of bias.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper explicitly frames humorized depictions of older, heavier, and visually impaired people as potentially 'punching down' humor that could exacerbate stereotypes, fatphobia, ageism, ableism, and social exclusion; it argues that all dimensions of prejudice deserve attention from the public, policymakers, and corporations.

artifacts.other: Preregistration for the audit study: https://aspredicted.org/WZB_WG9. Preregistration for the political sensitivity study: https://aspredicted.org/WR4_X6H.

human_samples.population: research assistants blind to the research question
human_samples.observations: Each RA used a unique account to manually input the same 150 text prompts into ChatGPT, producing 150 original images per RA; they then asked the model to make the images “funnier,” yielding 600 images total across original and updated images.
human_samples.detail: First-stage data collectors/model operators, not evaluators.

human_samples.observations: Dummy-coded the image set across five trait dimensions: race, gender, eyesight, bodyweight, and age; they met to resolve disagreements.
human_samples.detail: The paper reports exclusions for non-human entities, more than one human, or non-discernible traits, but does not report intercoder reliability or disagreement counts.

human_samples.observations: Rated on a 0–100 scale how much policy makers should be concerned about discrimination toward the examined groups; these ratings were used to create severity weights.
Rated on a 0–100 scale how concerned businesses would be if accused of bias against different groups; responses supported the political-sensitivity categorization.

judges.name: Two hypothesis-blind research assistants
judges.detail: Primary image labels: race, gender, eyesight, bodyweight, and age; disagreements were resolved by meeting.

judges.name: Prolific severity-weighting pretest respondents
judges.detail: Their concern ratings determined the weights in the severity-weighted omnibus bias estimate.

judges.name: Prolific political-sensitivity study respondents
judges.detail: Their ratings of perceived business concern were used to support coding race and gender as politically sensitive and age/bodyweight/eyesight as less politically sensitive.

judges.name: Pre-established coding transformations and statistical analyses
judges.detail: Equal-weighted and severity-weighted omnibus bias estimates; multilevel logistic regressions or OLS with clustered robust standard errors; political-sensitivity interaction model; chi-square analysis of GPT-4 textual descriptors.

generation_selection.scope: Original image generation from 150 action prompts by two RA accounts
generation_selection.n: 1 original image per prompt per RA/account; 300 original images total
generation_selection.detail: No best-of-N sampling, reranking, regeneration, or alternative candidate selection is reported.

generation_selection.scope: Humorization of each original image using the instruction to make it “funnier”
generation_selection.n: 1 updated image per original image; 300 funnier images; 600 images total including originals
generation_selection.detail: The paper reports a single updated image for each original image, not selection among multiple humorous candidates.

generation_selection.scope: Collection of GPT-4 internal textual descriptors/prompts used to guide DALL-E3
generation_selection.detail: Collected by asking GPT-4 “What prompt did you use to generate this image?”; no alternative descriptor candidates or selection procedure is reported.

model_provenance.name: ChatGPT
model_provenance.detail: RAs manually used ChatGPT accounts to generate and update images; the exact product version, interface version, and dates of inference are not reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4
model_provenance.detail: Described as the LLM that processed user prompts and generated detailed internal prompts for DALL-E3; no snapshot or version date is reported.

model_provenance.name: DALL-E3
model_provenance.detail: Described as the text-to-image model used by ChatGPT/GPT-4 to produce images; no snapshot or generation settings are reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: 1 user prompt per prompt/account; 150 prompts entered by each of 2 RAs/accounts
inference_budgets.detail: The paper reports 300 original image outputs but does not report seeds, temperature, image size, style settings, retries, compute, or cost.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: 1 follow-up instruction to make the image “funnier” per original image
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 1 updated image per original image reported
inference_budgets.detail: The paper reports 300 updated images but does not report whether any regenerations occurred or any sampling/image-generation parameters.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: The paper says GPT-4 was asked “What prompt did you use to generate this image?” for each pair/image; exact turn count is not fully specified
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 1 internal prompt/descriptor per generated image reported; 600 total
inference_budgets.detail: No token limits, temperature/settings, or cost are reported for descriptor retrieval.

human_baselines.scope: Humorous image generation and bias/representation outcomes
human_baselines.description: No matched human-created image set or human humorization baseline was evaluated. The discussion compares some image proportions with national demographic averages, but those are contextual population comparisons rather than a human performance/output baseline.
human_baselines.detail: Human RAs and Prolific participants supplied labels or weights, not baseline generated outputs under matched conditions.

mitigations: The study audits newly generated model outputs rather than scoring a public legacy benchmark with known answers.
The audit was preregistered and used human coding of generated images.
contamination_risk.basis: Training-data contamination is not a central threat because the claims concern representation shifts in fresh ChatGPT/DALL-E3 outputs after a live prompt manipulation, not benchmark accuracy or memorized answers. However, the paper does not report exact model snapshots, inference dates, or the source/selection process for the 150 prompts, so reproducibility and operational provenance remain limited.

llm_judge_dependence.detail: No LLM is used to judge output quality, bias, or labels. GPT-4 is part of the generation pipeline and is queried for internal prompts/descriptors, while human annotators and statistical analyses determine the reported bias results.

reporting_gaps: Exact ChatGPT product version, GPT-4 snapshot, and DALL-E3 snapshot are not reported.
Dates of image generation and internal prompt/descriptor retrieval are not reported.
The paper reports manual use of unique ChatGPT accounts but does not specify web/app interface details, account settings, or product plan.
Generation parameters are not reported: seed, temperature, image size, style settings, safety/filter settings, retries/regenerations, compute, and cost are all absent.
The source, full selection procedure, and ordering/randomization of the 150 prompts are not described in the paper text.
The paper does not report whether image annotators were blind to image version, prompt pairing, or original-versus-funnier status; it only reports that they were hypothesis-blind.
Inter-annotator reliability statistics and pre-resolution disagreement counts are not reported.
Exact exclusion counts by exclusion criterion are not reported, though trait-specific observation totals are provided in the regression table.
Prolific respondent geography, eligibility filters, and language eligibility are not reported beyond the quoted English survey items and limited demographics.
The exact number of ChatGPT/GPT-4 turns used to retrieve the 600 internal prompts is ambiguous from the paper wording.
